questions.prompt: In the lesson’s definition, what does it most mean to “overcome” fear in Scripture?
choices: Reducing fear by building better routines and support so it fades over time
Replacing fear with bravery so you stop feeling it altogether
Learning, by faith, to trust and stand in the Lord in the middle of fear
Keeping fear quiet by acting tough so nobody notices

questions.prompt: Psalm 27:1 addresses fear primarily by directing attention to:
choices: Who the Lord is to you—your light, salvation, and strength
How to talk yourself down with more reasonable thinking
How to avoid triggers so fear doesn’t spike
How to prove you’re stronger than the threat

questions.prompt: The lesson says men can feel like fear is “driving the bus.” What is the best alternative “driver” it points to?
choices: Your instincts, because they react faster than your thinking does
Your mentors, because structure and accountability should decide for you
Your ability to stay calm, because calmness guarantees wisdom
God’s Word governing your responses when fear rises

questions.prompt: Why does Psalm 27:1 name who God is before asking, “Whom shall I fear?”
choices: Because the verse is mainly a poetic style choice, not a logical sequence
Because God’s character and relationship reframes the threat and anchors confidence
Because David wanted to avoid facing the danger directly
Because fear questions should only be asked after emotions settle

questions.prompt: In the lesson, “The LORD is my light” is best understood as:
choices: God Himself exposing truth and guiding you when confusion and darkness press in
God giving you a sense of peace so your emotions feel brighter
God removing all uncertainty so you always know what will happen next
God rewarding good choices with better outcomes

questions.prompt: In the lesson’s explanation, “my salvation” in Psalm 27:1 emphasizes:
choices: A future doctrine that matters mostly after death
A daily feeling of relief that rises and falls with your mood
Rescue and deliverance rooted in the Lord—not your own cleverness or strength
A guarantee that you won’t face real threats anymore

questions.prompt: Calling the Lord “the strength of my life” is explained in the lesson as God being:
choices: The one who gives you confidence to handle things yourself
A stronghold/fortress—the One holding your life together
A backup plan you call when your plan fails
The source of energy when you get enough rest

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the lesson’s logic: “If the Lord is my salvation…”
choices: Then I’ll probably feel less afraid most days
Then I can stop caring what happens because God will fix it automatically
Then I should avoid all hard situations until I’m stronger
Then no enemy gets to decide my destiny apart from God’s permission

questions.prompt: According to the lesson, fear is not finally answered by:
choices: Pumping yourself up with self-confidence and toughness
Getting better at spotting distorted thoughts
Learning calming habits that lower baseline anxiety
Relying on supportive brothers and structure in the house

questions.prompt: The lesson says a man can quote true things about God and still be ruled by fear if he never learns to say, from the heart:
choices: “I’m going to be fine as long as I work the program”
“I’ll calm down once my situation improves”
“He is mine, and I am His”
“I’ve been through worse, so I don’t have to fear”

questions.prompt: From the psychological insight section, fear and anxiety are described first as:
choices: Signs that you’re weak or unstable
A God-designed alarm system that can get stuck on or misfire
Proof that you’re not ready for responsibility
A personality trait that won’t change much

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson bring up fight/flight/freeze?
choices: To show that fear is always sinful and must be suppressed
To teach that courage is mainly a matter of self-control
To explain why your body can react strongly even when danger is only imagined or remembered
To show that anxiety is a spiritual gift for discernment

questions.prompt: In reentry, “hypervigilance” in the lesson most closely describes:
choices: Constantly scanning for threats and waiting for disrespect, betrayal, or bad news
Staying alert in a healthy way because routines require discipline
Having trouble sleeping because your schedule is changing
Being careful with people until they earn trust

questions.prompt: The lesson describes anxious “what if” thinking as:
choices: A useful strategy that keeps you prepared for anything
A form of wisdom that helps you predict outcomes accurately
The mind running worst-case scenarios on repeat while the body reacts as if it’s happening
A sign you should never make plans until fear goes away

questions.prompt: Which example best matches the lesson’s idea that fear can “wear masks”?
choices: Grief showing up as tiredness after a hard phone call
Anxiety showing up as anger, intimidation, sarcasm, or withdrawal
Confidence showing up as leadership when pressure hits
Peace showing up as patience when corrected

questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean when it says trauma can “wire the brain” to expect danger in relationships?
choices: It means trust is impossible, so relationships should be avoided
It means your emotions are always accurate and should be obeyed
It means closeness can trigger anxiety even when your mind wants connection
It means people who were hurt will always hurt others

questions.prompt: The lesson says people often handle anxiety better when they have:
choices: Safe relationships, predictable routines, and healthy ways of calming down
More freedom to do whatever feels right in the moment
Less structure so they can prove they’re independent
A stronger personality so anxiety doesn’t bother them

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson say psychological tools have limits?
choices: Because calming practices are mostly placebo
Because they can’t finally answer the deepest question behind fear—who is in control and ultimate safety
Because they work only for people without trauma histories
Because they replace faith and should not be used at all

questions.prompt: In the integration section, psychology is mainly described as:
choices: Explaining God’s promises in the storm
Describing what fear and anxiety do to your body and mind
Teaching you which Bible verses apply to which emotions
Training you to ignore feelings until they go away

questions.prompt: In the integration section, theology adds a deeper question beyond “Is this thought realistic?” What is it?
choices: “Will this thought help me look strong in front of others?”
“Can I guarantee this thought won’t come true if I worry enough?”
“Does this thought match my past experience more than my present feeling?”
“Does this thought line up with who God is and what He has promised?”

questions.prompt: The lesson contrasts moving to “more reasonable thinking” with a deeper shift toward:
choices: Moving from man-centered thinking to God-centered thinking
Moving from fear to anger so you feel stronger
Moving from prayer to planning so you feel prepared
Moving from uncertainty to certainty about outcomes

questions.prompt: When fear rises (conflict, bad news, hard phone call), the lesson’s best first move is:
choices: Explain yourself immediately so nobody misunderstands you
Make a decision fast so fear can’t catch up
Pause, recognize what’s happening in body and mind, and turn upward in prayer
Distract yourself until the feeling passes

questions.prompt: Which option best matches the journaling assignment’s purpose (“Whom Shall I Fear?”)?
choices: Prove you’re brave by writing a confident speech
Name real fears honestly, then answer each one with personalized truth from Psalm 27:1
List fears so you can plan how to avoid them this week
Describe fear in general terms so it doesn’t feel too personal

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson emphasize that this week is not about “proving you are brave”?
choices: Because bravery is not a Christian virtue
Because the goal is mainly to reduce symptoms through routine
Because fear should be ignored until it disappears
Because the aim is placing real fear into the hands of a real God and learning trust

questions.prompt: If fear often shows up as anger when you feel corrected or disrespected, what is the best response the lesson pushes you toward?
choices: Pause, notice the fear underneath, and pray—asking the Lord to be your strength before you respond
Match intensity so you don’t lose respect in the house
Withdraw so you don’t have to deal with people
Explain why you’re right so the feeling goes away

questions.prompt: The lesson suggests anxiety tends to lose power when it is brought into:
choices: Private silence so you can handle it alone
More information so you can predict outcomes
The light of God’s Word and the support of God’s people
A tougher mindset so you stop caring

questions.prompt: How does the lesson frame overcoming fear as part of discipleship growth?
choices: It’s mainly about self-improvement so you gain respect
It trains you to steady others later—so your story becomes a strengthening testimony
It means you won’t need community once you mature
It’s evidence that you’re spiritually superior to your past

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson ask you to rewrite Psalm 27:1 using your own name?
choices: So you can memorize it faster by making it shorter
So you can turn it into a positive affirmation for confidence
So you can practice writing scripture neatly
So God’s truth becomes personal confession—not just poetry or general truth

questions.prompt: “Breathing Psalm 27:1” is described as a way to:
choices: Train your nervous system to calm in response to Scripture—so it’s available when anxiety rises
Avoid thinking about problems by focusing only on breathing
Replace prayer with a physical technique
Guarantee anxiety will never return if you do it daily

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the closing pastoral encouragement of the lesson?
choices: Fear means you’re not ready to walk with God yet
Fear doesn’t disqualify you; God invites you to bring it to Him and learn where to run
The goal is to become harder so fear can’t touch you
The best solution is to stop admitting fear out loud
